input.background: A citizen claims that the intelligence of children in a particular community is above average. To test this claim, he selects a simple random sample consisting of n=9 children from this population. Scores of the intelligence test typically vary according to a Normal distribution with mean μ=100 and standard deviation σ=15. The sample produces these scores {116,128,125,119,89,99,105,116,118}. Thus, x̄=(116+128+⋯+118)/9=112.8.
input.question: Does this sample provide statistically significant evidence in support of the citizen's claim?

output.answer: A. Hypotheses. Since 100 represents the expected population average if no difference were to exist, H0: μ=100. The one-sided alternative hypothesis is Ha: μ>100. The two-sided alternative is Ha: μ≠100.

sample_size.description: The size of the random sample selected from the population.

sample_scores.description: The intelligence test scores of the sampled children.

sample_mean.description: The mean intelligence test score of the sampled children.

population_mean.description: The mean intelligence test score of the population under the null hypothesis.

population_sd.description: The standard deviation of intelligence test scores in the population.
